examples.title: Project Constraint Balance Check
examples.description: Algorithm to check if project constraints are balanced
examples.code:
BEGIN
 INPUT scope_size
 INPUT timeline_weeks
 INPUT budget_amount
 INPUT resource_count
 
 // Calculate constraint ratios
 efficiency_ratio = scope_size / (timeline_weeks * resource_count)
 budget_per_week = budget_amount / timeline_weeks
 
 // Check balance thresholds
 IF efficiency_ratio > 1.5 THEN
 OUTPUT "Warning: Scope too large for resources and time"
 OUTPUT "Recommendation: Reduce scope or increase resources/time"
 ELSE IF budget_per_week < (scope_size / 10) THEN
 OUTPUT "Warning: Budget insufficient for planned scope"
 OUTPUT "Recommendation: Increase budget or reduce scope"
 ELSE
 OUTPUT "Project constraints appear balanced"
 ENDIF
 
 RETURN balance_status
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates a basic constraint balancing check. In real projects, more sophisticated algorithms consider risk, quality metrics, and stakeholder priorities.

examples.title: Stakeholder Analysis Process
examples.description: Process for identifying and categorizing stakeholders
examples.code:
BEGIN
 stakeholders = []
 
 FUNCTION identify_stakeholders()
 ADD all team members TO stakeholders
 ADD all clients/customers TO stakeholders
 ADD all sponsors TO stakeholders
 ADD all users TO stakeholders
 ADD regulatory bodies TO stakeholders
 RETURN stakeholders
 END FUNCTION
 
 FUNCTION categorize_stakeholders(stakeholders)
 FOR EACH person IN stakeholders
 power = assess_power(person)
 interest = assess_interest(person)
 
 IF power = HIGH AND interest = HIGH THEN
 category = "Manage Closely"
 ELSE IF power = HIGH AND interest = LOW THEN
 category = "Keep Satisfied"
 ELSE IF power = LOW AND interest = HIGH THEN
 category = "Keep Informed"
 ELSE
 category = "Monitor"
 ENDIF
 
 person.category = category
 END FOR
 RETURN stakeholders
 END FUNCTION
 
 stakeholder_list = identify_stakeholders()
 categorized = categorize_stakeholders(stakeholder_list)
 OUTPUT categorized
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm systematically identifies stakeholders and categorizes them based on power and interest levels, enabling appropriate engagement strategies.

examples.title: Scope Change Control Process
examples.description: Process to manage scope creep through formal change control
examples.code:
BEGIN
 FUNCTION handle_scope_change(change_request)
 // Step 1: Log the change request
 log_request(change_request)
 
 // Step 2: Assess impact on constraints
 impact_scope = calculate_scope_impact(change_request)
 impact_time = calculate_time_impact(change_request)
 impact_cost = calculate_cost_impact(change_request)
 impact_risk = calculate_risk_impact(change_request)
 
 // Step 3: Evaluate if change is feasible
 IF (impact_time > available_slack) OR (impact_cost > available_budget) THEN
 OUTPUT "Change requires trade-off negotiation"
 CALL negotiate_constraints(change_request)
 ENDIF
 
 // Step 4: Get approval if within limits
 IF (impact_within_limits) THEN
 approved = get_approval(stakeholders)
 IF approved THEN
 UPDATE project_plan(change_request)
 OUTPUT "Change implemented successfully"
 ELSE
 OUTPUT "Change rejected"
 ENDIF
 ELSE
 OUTPUT "Change rejected - exceeds constraints"
 ENDIF
 
 END FUNCTION
 
 RETURN status
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode implements a formal change control process to prevent scope creep by evaluating impacts on all constraints and requiring proper approval.
